policy_statement: Stimulate increased production and use of biofuels as an alternative to fossil fuels.
mechanism_description: The policy stimulates greater biofuel production and use so biofuels substitute for fossil fuels, lowering emissions from fossil energy consumption.
primary_causal_pathway: Fuel switching (fossil → biofuel) → ↓ lifecycle net GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changed activity/system: Energy supply and end-use fuel mix shifts toward greater biofuel production, distribution, and consumption (often in transport fuels), reducing demand for gasoline/diesel or other fossil fuels. (2) Mediating process: Combustion emissions are partially offset by biogenic carbon uptake during biomass growth and/or by using waste-derived/advanced feedstocks; overall lifecycle emissions per unit energy can be lower than fossil fuels depending on feedstock, processing energy, and land-use effects. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in GHG emissions (CO₂e) from fuel combustion and upstream fuel-cycle emissions due to fossil fuel displacement.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is displacement of fossil fuel use with lower-net-GHG biofuels, reducing net greenhouse gas emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s core logic is fuel switching that reduces net lifecycle GHG emissions by displacing fossil fuels.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While biofuels can sometimes be framed as “resource efficiency” (use of wastes) or “nature-based” (biomass growth), the dominant intended climate effect across policies using this mechanism is direct mitigation via lower net emissions from the energy system.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some biofuel pathways can increase emissions (e.g., high ILUC, deforestation, peat impacts, high-fossil process energy) or shift benefits toward waste management; those are implementation/quality contingencies and do not change the primary mechanism category, but they affect real-world efficacy.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose mechanism is explicitly “biofuel expansion to displace fossil fuels” is fundamentally aimed at reducing GHG emissions from energy consumption, i.e., mitigation.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further refinement (e.g., “transport decarbonization,” “advanced waste-based biofuels,” “LCFS”) depends on policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level coding.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Lifecycle outcomes depend strongly on feedstock and land-use change; if biofuel expansion causes deforestation/peat conversion or major indirect land-use change, net emissions can rise despite fossil displacement. Sustainability criteria and carbon-intensity standards are often needed to ensure mitigation benefits.
key_indicators: “Stimulating”; “increased production and use of biofuels”; “alternative to fossil fuels”
co_benefits: Energy security; rural/agricultural income; air quality improvements (potentially)
instance_edge_case_notes: The instrument is not explicitly specified (could also be implemented via blending mandates, standards, procurement, or infrastructure investment), but “stimulating” most strongly signals incentives/support rather than regulation or target-setting.
